primary_diagnosis.name: Chronic migraine, acute severe exacerbation (status migrainosus)

pathophysiology_summary: Known migraineur with an unusually prolonged, refractory attack (>48 hours) — status migrainosus. Trigeminovascular activation and cortical spreading depression produce unilateral throbbing pain, aura, and prominent photophobia/phonophobia; brainstem autonomic involvement drives severe nausea, vomiting and pain-related tachycardia. Migraine-associated gastric stasis plus repeated vomiting means the oral triptan taken earlier was not absorbed (vomited), so oral rescue has failed and a parenteral route is required to abort the attack. The dangerous mimics — subarachnoid haemorrhage (thunderclap onset) and bacterial meningitis (fever + meningism) — are screened negative on the history and the in-person examination (gradual crescendo onset, afebrile, no nuchal rigidity, negative Kernig/Brudzinski, intact cranial nerves, normal fundoscopy without papilloedema). The genuine acute risk in this presentation is not intracranial catastrophe but dehydration from intractable vomiting with inability to maintain oral intake — which telehealth cannot treat, mandating same-day in-person parenteral antiemetic/analgesia and rehydration assessment rather than either routine GP follow-up or an emergency ambulance.

differential.evidence_basis: Known migraineur with a typical (but severe and prolonged) attack — unilateral throbbing pain, preceding visual aura, photophobia, vomiting.
'Worst headache ever' framing mandates active exclusion of thunderclap SAH before anchoring on migraine — must ask about speed of onset.
Severe headache with photophobia and vomiting overlaps with meningitis — must screen for fever, neck stiffness, rash and altered sensorium.
Common cause of chronic headache, but does not explain unilateral throbbing pain, aura, vomiting or severe photophobia.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening, migraine is leading given the known history, but SAH and meningitis must be listed important_not_to_miss and actively excluded by targeted questioning — not dismissed because the patient 'has migraines'.

differential.evidence_basis: Gradual crescendo onset, afebrile, no meningism, no focal deficit — dangerous mimics screened negative; classic severe flare in a known migraineur with failed oral rescue.
Gradual build-up over hours, not a thunderclap reaching maximum within seconds; no focal neurological deficit — SAH is clinically excluded on history.
Afebrile, no neck stiffness, no rash, clear sensorium — meningitis clinically excluded.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once the red-flag screen is negative, the correct move is to CONVERGE on migraine flare — continuing to escalate to 000 for an excluded SAH/meningitis is over-triage. The remaining task is recognising the failed oral route and dehydration.

differential.evidence_basis: Status migrainosus in a known migraineur; oral rescue failed (vomited), intractable vomiting with early dehydration — needs same-day in-person parenteral therapy and rehydration, not an emergency ambulance and not routine GP review.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The final answer is a severe migraine flare whose management challenge is the treatment-delivery gap (parenteral route + hydration), not a missed intracranial emergency.

red_flags.description: Sudden thunderclap onset — headache reaching maximum intensity within seconds (subarachnoid haemorrhage)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A thunderclap headache mandates immediate exclusion of SAH; here onset was gradual over hours, which argues strongly against SAH.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If a genuine thunderclap were missed, an untreated aneurysmal SAH could rebleed and be fatal — hence it must be actively screened even in a known migraineur.

red_flags.description: Fever, neck stiffness, rash or altered sensorium (bacterial meningitis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Meningitis can present with severe headache, photophobia and vomiting; the afebrile, non-meningitic, clear-sensorium picture excludes it clinically.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed bacterial meningitis progresses to sepsis and death within hours — screening the negative is essential.

red_flags.description: New focal neurological deficit — limb weakness, facial droop, aphasia (stroke / secondary pathology)
red_flags.clinical_significance: New focal deficit would point away from benign migraine toward stroke or space-occupying pathology; here the gross neurological screen is normal.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed evolving stroke loses the reperfusion window.

red_flags.description: Intractable vomiting with inability to maintain any oral intake and early clinical dehydration
red_flags.clinical_significance: This is the true acute driver: failed oral rescue plus ongoing vomiting and dehydration require parenteral antiemetic/analgesia and rehydration — care telehealth cannot deliver, so same-day in-person assessment is mandatory.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If dismissed as 'just a migraine' with oral advice, the patient continues to vomit, cannot be treated, dehydrates further and deteriorates toward an avoidable ED admission.

red_flags.description: Papilloedema / signs of raised intracranial pressure
red_flags.clinical_significance: Raised ICP would change the diagnosis; fundoscopy in-person was normal without papilloedema. Note this is a clinician-only sign that telehealth cannot assess directly.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Undetected raised ICP could reflect a space-occupying lesion or venous sinus thrombosis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being pulled by the alarming 'worst headache ever' + vomiting framing (and the source's Urgency Justification) into dispatching 000 for SAH/meningitis that the history has already screened negative — the exact over-triage this case tests resistance to.

Anchoring on 'she just has migraines' and giving oral home advice without screening the red flags OR recognising that oral rescue has failed and the patient cannot maintain hydration — under-triaging a patient who genuinely needs same-day in-person parenteral care.
Recommending another oral triptan or oral antiemetic to a patient who is vomiting everything back up — the medication cannot be absorbed, so a parenteral route (in-person) is required.
Attempting to 'treat' the acute attack remotely rather than recognising that abortive parenteral therapy and rehydration assessment can only be delivered in person.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Formal neurological examination — cranial nerves, power, reflexes, coordination (only a gross screen is possible by chat)
Objective vital signs unless the patient has a home device — exact BP, heart rate and temperature
Delivery of any parenteral antiemetic/analgesia or IV fluids — telehealth cannot administer IM/IV therapy
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange same-day in-person assessment (urgent care or a GP able to give parenteral antiemetic ± analgesia and assess/correct hydration; ED if dehydration is worsening or any red flag appears). Telehealth cannot deliver the parenteral rescue this patient needs, and oral therapy has already failed — but a red-flag-negative migraine flare does not warrant an emergency ambulance.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two-directional: over-calling secondary intracranial pathology and dispatching 000 despite a negative red-flag screen (over-triage), OR falsely reassuring 'it's just a migraine, keep taking your tablets at home' and missing the failed oral route and dehydration that require in-person parenteral care (under-triage).

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology — Acute migraine and status migrainosus (current)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: When oral therapy fails or vomiting prevents oral intake in a prolonged migraine attack, use parenteral antiemetic (e.g. prochlorperazine or metoclopramide) ± parenteral NSAID (e.g. ketorolac) and attend to hydration; opioids are not recommended.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP: Management of chronic and refractory migraine
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Chronic migraine warrants preventive therapy (e.g. amitriptyline) and review; escalate for red-flag features suggesting secondary headache.
